examples.title: Checking Universal Statement
examples.description: Algorithm to check if ∀x P(x) is true for a finite domain
examples.code:
FUNCTION CheckUniversal(domain, predicate P):
  FOR EACH element x IN domain:
    IF NOT P(x) THEN
      RETURN FALSE
    END IF
  END FOR
  RETURN TRUE

// Example: domain = {2,3,5,7}, P(x) = isPrime(x)
// Returns TRUE as all elements are prime
examples.explanation: This algorithm iterates through every element in the domain and checks if the predicate P holds for each. If any element fails, the universal statement is false. This implements the definition: ∀x P(x) is true iff P(x) is true for all x in the domain.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is the size of the domain

examples.title: Checking Existential Statement
examples.description: Algorithm to check if ∃x P(x) is true for a finite domain
examples.code:
FUNCTION CheckExistential(domain, predicate P):
  FOR EACH element x IN domain:
    IF P(x) THEN
      RETURN TRUE
    END IF
  END FOR
  RETURN FALSE

// Example: domain = {1,2,3,4,5}, P(x) = (x > 4)
// Returns TRUE as x=5 satisfies the predicate
examples.explanation: This algorithm searches for at least one element in the domain that satisfies the predicate. As soon as one is found, it returns true. This implements the definition: ∃x P(x) is true iff there exists at least one x in the domain such that P(x) is true.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) in worst case, O(1) in best case

examples.title: Negating Quantified Statements
examples.description: Python functions to compute negation of universal and existential statements
examples.code:
def negate_universal(domain, predicate):
    """
    Returns True if ¬∀x P(x) is true, i.e., ∃x ¬P(x)
    Equivalent to: there exists an element where predicate is False
    """
    return any(not predicate(x) for x in domain)

def negate_existential(domain, predicate):
    """
    Returns True if ¬∃x P(x) is true, i.e., ∀x ¬P(x)
    Equivalent to: predicate is False for all elements
    """
    return all(not predicate(x) for x in domain)

# Example usage:
numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
is_positive = lambda x: x > 0
print(negate_universal(numbers, is_positive))  # False (all are positive)
print(negate_existential(numbers, is_positive)) # False (some are positive)
examples.explanation: The functions implement the quantifier negation rules. negate_universal finds if there's any counterexample (∃x ¬P(x)), and negate_existential checks if no element satisfies the predicate (∀x ¬P(x)).

examples.title: Universal Modus Ponens Proof
examples.description: Demonstrates formal proof using universal modus ponens
examples.explanation: This algorithm demonstrates the formal proof of universal modus ponens. It uses Universal Instantiation to get specific instances, applies Modus Ponens to derive Q(a), then uses Universal Generalization to conclude ∀x Q(x).
